title: Pip and the Lost Cloud
author: Fablewick
lesson: Helping someone get home is the kindest kind of work.
summary: A small cloud has wandered too far. Pip the field mouse knows the way back.
coverIllustration: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><ellipse cx='100' cy='70' rx='45' ry='22' fill='#FFFCF6' opacity='0.95'/><ellipse cx='75' cy='65' rx='22' ry='15' fill='#FFFCF6' opacity='0.9'/><ellipse cx='120' cy='62' rx='25' ry='17' fill='#FFFCF6' opacity='0.92'/><circle cx='100' cy='120' r='8' fill='#B26F3D'/><circle cx='95' cy='118' r='1.5' fill='#2A2118'/><circle cx='105' cy='118' r='1.5' fill='#2A2118'/><path d='M97 122 Q100 124 103 122' stroke='#2A2118' stroke-width='1' fill='none'/><ellipse cx='90' cy='115' rx='3' ry='5' fill='#B26F3D'/><ellipse cx='110' cy='115' rx='3' ry='5' fill='#B26F3D'/></svg>

1.label: Little Listener
1.text:
Pip was a small, small mouse. Pip lived in the long, long grass.

One morning, Pip heard a tiny voice.

“I am lost,” said the voice. “I am lost, lost, lost.”

Pip looked up. A little white cloud! Down low, bumping into a thistle. Bump, bump, bump.

“Little cloud,” said Pip, “you are far from the sky.”

“I came down to see a poppy,” said the cloud. “It was so red. But now I can’t find my way up.”

Pip thought. Then Pip climbed. Up the tall, tall grass. Up, up, up, to the very tippy top.

Pip pointed with one small paw.

“Look! There go the clouds. There goes your family!”

The little cloud looked. And looked. And — there they were! Soft and white, drifting along.

The cloud took a slow, soft breath.

And up went the cloud. Up past the grass. Up past the wall. Up, up, up, into the big blue sky!

The cloud waved one soft white wave.

Pip waved back with both small paws.

“Goodbye, little cloud,” said Pip. “Welcome home.”

2.label: New Reader
2.text:
Pip was a small field mouse who lived between a barley field and an old stone wall.

One quiet morning, Pip was nibbling a fine piece of clover when a tiny voice floated by.

“I am lost,” it said. “I do not know how to get home.”

Pip looked up.

There, just above the tallest grasses, hung a small white cloud. It bumped gently into a thistle, the way a balloon bumps into a ceiling. Bump. Bump.

“Excuse me,” said Pip. “Are you a cloud?”

“Yes,” sniffed the cloud. “I came down to look at a poppy. It was so red. I had never seen anything so red. But now I can’t find my way back to the sky.”

“Clouds belong up high,” said Pip gently.

“I know,” said the cloud, very small. “But every time I try, I go sideways. Or down. Never up. I just want to go home.”

Pip looked at the grass, and the wall, and the wide blue morning.

Then Pip had an idea.

Pip climbed, carefully, carefully, to the top of the tallest stalk of grass in the whole field. From up there, Pip could see everything. And high above, far away, a slow line of other clouds drifted across the sky.

Pip pointed with one small paw.

“There,” said Pip. “There is your family. They’re going that way.”

The cloud looked. And looked. And then — it saw them, soft and white, sailing across the blue.

The cloud took a slow, soft breath.

And very gently, it began to lift. Just a little at first, like dandelion fluff catching the wind.

Then a little more.

Then up, and up, and up — all the way into the bright blue sky.

The cloud waved a soft white wave.

Pip stood on top of the tall grass and waved back, both paws high above his head.

“Goodbye, little cloud!” Pip called. “Welcome home!”

The cloud slipped into the line of its family, and together they drifted out of sight.

Pip climbed back down to his clover.

It was, Pip thought, a very good morning.

3.label: On Your Own
3.text:
Pip lived in the long grass between a barley field and an old stone wall. Pip was a small field mouse with a slightly chipped front tooth and a very serious little face. Pip’s days went mostly the same way: collect seeds, listen for owls, nap in the warm part of the afternoon.

Until the morning Pip met the cloud.

It was just after sunrise. Dew hung on every blade of grass like a row of tiny lamps. Pip was halfway through breakfast, a particularly excellent piece of clover, when a small voice drifted down through the green.

“I am lost,” said the voice. “I do not know how to get home.”

Pip stopped chewing.

There, just above the grass-tops, bumping gently into a thistle and looking rather embarrassed about it, hung a small, soft, perfectly round white cloud.

“Hello,” said Pip, carefully. “You are a cloud.”

“Yes,” said the cloud, in a voice that sounded a bit watery.

“You are very close to the ground.”

“I know.” The cloud sniffled. “I came down to look at a poppy. It was so red. I had never seen anything so red in my whole life. I only meant to look for a moment.” A pause. “But now I can’t find the way back. I keep drifting sideways. Once I drifted into the wall. It was very firm.”

Pip considered the cloud. It was about half the size of a good mouse-hole, and it was the gentlest thing Pip had ever seen up close.

“Clouds belong up there,” Pip said. “The sky is up.”

“I know,” said the cloud, smaller still. “But I have forgotten how to go up. Every time I try, I go the wrong way. I just want to be home with my family.”

This, Pip understood. Pip had once gotten turned around in a thick stand of barley and spent a whole afternoon walking the wrong direction, certain the entire time that it was the burrow that had moved.

So Pip looked around. Pip thought.

Then Pip had what felt like a very good idea.

“Come on,” said Pip. “Follow me. But not too close. I think the thistles might be a problem for you.”

Pip climbed. Up the old wall, where the moss grew thick and easy to grip. Up the longest stalk of grass at the wall’s edge, the one that swayed when even the smallest breeze went by. Higher and higher, until the wind turned crisp and the field spread out below like a green carpet rolling away in every direction.

The cloud followed, drifting awkwardly, bumping the stalk twice.

From the very top, Pip pointed with one small paw.

“Look,” said Pip. “Out there. Do you see them?”

The cloud looked.

Far, far up — much higher than the grass, much higher than the wall — a long, lazy line of clouds moved slowly across the morning sky. Some were big. Some were small. All of them were going somewhere together.

For a long moment, the little cloud said nothing.

Then, very quietly: “That’s them.”

“That’s them,” Pip agreed.

The cloud took a slow, soft breath.

And very gently, like a piece of dandelion fluff that has finally made up its mind, the cloud began to lift.

A little. Then a little more. Then more.

Up past the grass. Up past the wall. Up past the highest branch of the old willow. Up and up and up, until it was just a small white circle against the blue, drifting toward the line of others.

Just before it caught up, the cloud paused.

And then it waved — a tiny curl at its edge, the smallest wave in the world. But Pip saw it.

Pip stood on the very top of the tallest stalk of grass and waved back with both paws.

“Goodbye, little cloud!” Pip called, though the cloud was much too far away to hear. “Welcome home!”

The cloud slipped into the line of its family, and together they all drifted away over the barley, toward wherever it is that clouds go in the morning.

Pip climbed down slowly, the way mice do.

The clover was still waiting at the bottom. It looked a little less impressive than before. Pip ate it anyway. It tasted, somehow, like a very good morning.

Later, when the sun was high and the whole field smelled of warm grass, Pip sat in the doorway of the burrow and watched the sky.

There were a great many clouds up there now. Pip could not tell which was which.

But Pip thought, and Pip was almost sure, that one of them was waving.
